title: Stripping wallpaper: steam and scorer method
subtitle: Score the face, steam the back, scrape clean
excerpt: How to strip wallpaper from a plastered wall using a wallpaper scorer and a steam stripper: scoring the face to let steam through, working in sections, reading the plaster surface as you go, and preparing the wall for the next finish.
sourceNotes: Standard UK wallpaper removal practice. Collins Complete DIY Manual. Steam stripper technique unchanged from its domestic introduction in the 1960s. Safety-hold time for a plastered wall before re-decoration from British Gypsum guidance.

recipeTools.notes: For larger areas of soaked paper.
For rubbing back the wall surface once stripped and dry.

content.text: Stripping wallpaper is physical work but not skilled work. The skill is in reading the wall as you go: noticing when plaster is beginning to come away with the paper, when a new approach is needed, and when to stop and let the wall dry before continuing. Old plaster and plasterboard both respond to over-wetting; the steam stripper is powerful, and patience with the scraper prevents damage.
What you'll need
Wallpaper scorer (paper tiger).
A spiked wheel tool that perforates the paper face without cutting the plaster, allowing steam to penetrate the paste behind.
Steam stripper.
Hire or buy. Fill the tank and allow it to reach working pressure before starting. Replace water when the indicator shows half-empty.
50 mm scraper.
For lifting the softened paper. A flexible blade is easier to control than a stiff one against varied plaster.
Cotton dust sheet.
Wet paper and paste fall in quantity; a dust sheet protects the floor and makes clean-up manageable.
Preparation
Step 1, protect electrics and furnishings.

Turn off the electricity at the consumer unit for the room being stripped if there are sockets or switches in the walls. Steam near live sockets is a shock risk. Cover the floor and any remaining furniture with the cotton dust sheet.
Step 2, score the paper.

Run the wallpaper scorer across the full surface of the wall in overlapping circles. The spikes perforate the paper face. Do not press so hard that the spikes drag furrows in the plaster below. On vinyl or foil-backed paper the scorer is essential; steam alone cannot penetrate these faces.
Stripping
Step 3, steam the first section.

Hold the steam plate flat against the wall at the top of the first section for 20 to 30 seconds. Move the plate to the next section and simultaneously begin scraping the first section while it is still hot. Work in 600 mm square sections, moving the steam plate ahead of the scraper. If the paper is not releasing cleanly, hold the steam plate for a further 10 seconds.
Step 4, scrape from the top down.

Work downward so falling wet paper and paste do not re-wet sections you are about to steam. Hold the scraper at a low angle, about 30 degrees to the wall, and slide it under the softened paper with steady forward pressure. Do not lever the scraper against the plaster; the cutting edge will gouge it.
Step 5, remove the backing layer.

Some wallpapers have a decorative face that peels off dry, leaving a paper backing. Steam and scrape the backing as a separate pass. This backing soaks faster than the full paper; reduce the steaming time.
After stripping
Step 6, wash down the walls.

Once all paper is off, wash the walls with warm water and a sponge to remove paste residue. Paste left on the wall causes paint to lift and new paper to delaminate. Rinse with clean water and allow the walls to dry for at least 48 hours before filling or painting; longer in cold or damp conditions.
Step 7, inspect and fill the wall.
Once dry, the plaster surface reveals any damage. Fill gouges and holes made during stripping with lightweight filler, allow to cure, and sand back. The wall is now ready for painting or papering.
Common mistakes
Plaster coming away with the paper.

Usually the paper was hung on unprimed plaster and the paste has bonded to the surface. Reduce the steam time and use a lighter touch with the scraper. Where the plaster has already failed, stop, allow the wall to dry, and repair the plaster separately.
Paper tearing into small fragments instead of lifting in sheets.
The paper was not scored, or the steam time was too short. Score the remaining areas and increase the steam time by 10 seconds per section.
